metadata.working_title: A Neural Speech Decoding Framework Leveraging Deep Learning and a Differentiable Speech Synthesizer for ECoG-Based Brain-Computer Interfaces

abstract_sketch.problem: Decoding human speech from neural signals is essential for brain-computer interface (BCI) technologies that aim to restore speech to patients with severe neurological deficits.
abstract_sketch.gap: Existing ECoG-to-speech pipelines are limited by data scarcity, rely heavily on non-causal operations that consume auditory feedback, produce non-naturalistic reconstructions, and rarely release open-source code or evaluate the right hemisphere.
abstract_sketch.approach: We present a two-stage framework that pairs an ECoG Decoder (implemented with 3D ResNet, 3D SWIN Transformer, or LSTM backbones) with a novel fully differentiable Speech Synthesizer. A companion speech-to-speech auto-encoder provides reference speech parameters used to guide ECoG Decoder training, circumventing data scarcity.
abstract_sketch.key_results: Across N=48 participants, the causal ResNet decoder achieves PCC=0.798 and SWIN achieves PCC=0.796 against ground-truth spectrograms, with no significant difference from their non-causal counterparts. Right-hemisphere decoding (ResNet PCC=0.790, SWIN PCC=0.781) is statistically indistinguishable from the left, and low-density grids are competitive with hybrid-density grids.
abstract_sketch.impact: The framework delivers reproducible, natural-sounding, causal ECoG-to-speech decoding suitable for real-time BCIs, is the first to establish feasibility of right-hemisphere speech prosthetics, and is released with open-source code to accelerate community progress.

sections.title: Introduction
content_bullets: Speech loss due to neurological deficits severely limits social and work life; BCI systems aim to restore communication.
ECoG recordings from epilepsy surgery patients provide high spatial-temporal cortical data during speech.
Two key challenges: limited training data and high variability of speech production.
Prior linear models achieve ~0.6 PCC; deep learning approaches (CNN, RNN) produce non-naturalistic or low-accuracy speech.
Most prior work uses non-causal models, limiting real-time use; open-source code is rare.
We propose a two-stage pipeline with a differentiable Speech Synthesizer and low-dimensional speech-parameter latent space.
We provide multiple ECoG decoder backbones (ResNet, SWIN, LSTM), both causal and non-causal.
We evaluate on 48 participants and demonstrate right-hemisphere decoding feasibility.
We release open-source code at https://xc1490.github.io/nsd/.

sections.title: Related Work
content_bullets: Linear models for ECoG-to-speech decoding reached roughly 0.6 PCC.
Deep learning approaches (convolutional, recurrent) with varied latent representations (articulatory kinematics, GAN random vectors, direct spectrograms).
Naturalistic speech generation using wavenet vocoders, GANs, unit selection.
Most approaches are non-causal and lack open-source code.
Prior studies mostly focused on left hemisphere language-dominant cortex.

subsections.title: ECoG-to-Speech Decoding Framework
content_bullets: Two components: ECoG Decoder and Speech Synthesizer.
Two-stage training: (1) pretrain Speech Encoder + Speech Synthesizer as auto-encoder on speech-only data; (2) train ECoG Decoder with supervised spectrogram and reference-parameter losses.
18 speech parameters per time step (pitch f0, voice weight alpha, loudness L, 6 formants f1-f6 with amplitudes, unvoice filter parameters, background noise).

subsections.title: Differentiable Speech Synthesizer
content_bullets: Voice pathway: 6 formant filters applied to harmonic excitation; unvoice pathway: broadband filter applied to white noise; soft mix with voice weight alpha_t.
N=6 formants (linguistics literature uses N=2 for intelligibility).
M=80 prototype filter parameters per filter; threshold frequency f_theta, slope a, baseline bandwidth b0 learned during training.
K bin spectral sampling: 256 for female, 512 for male participants.
Total learnable parameters: M(N+1)=560 + 21 + K.

subsections.title: ECoG Decoder Architectures
content_bullets: Three backbones: 3D ResNet, 3D SWIN Transformer, LSTM; each implemented with causal and non-causal variants.
3D SWIN uses 2x2x2 patches as tokens; patch merging applies 2x2x2 down-sampling per stage.
ResNet downsamples electrode dimension to 1x1 and temporal to T/16, followed by transposed convolutions.

subsections.title: Data Collection and Preprocessing
content_bullets: N=48 native English speakers (26 female, 22 male) with refractory epilepsy at NYU Langone.
43 low-density (LD) 8x8 64-contact grids (10 mm spacing), 5 hybrid-density (HB) grids with 64 additional 1 mm electrodes at 5 mm spacing.
Left hemisphere: 32 participants; right hemisphere: 16 participants.
Five speech tasks (AR, AN, SC, WR/VR, PN); 50 target words; 400 trials/participant; 500 ms average speech duration.
ECoG sampled at 2048 Hz, down-sampled to 512 Hz; Hilbert transform for high gamma (70-150 Hz) envelope down-sampled to 125 Hz.
250 ms silent baseline for z-scoring; artifact and interictal electrodes excluded.
Praat used to estimate pitch and 4 formants as reference parameters; resampled to 125 Hz.

subsections.title: Training and Loss Functions
content_bullets: Reference loss weights: voice weight lambda_alpha=1.8, loudness lambda_L=1.5, pitch lambda_f0=0.4, formant frequencies lambda_f1=3, lambda_f2=1.8, lambda_f3=1.2, lambda_f4=0.9, lambda_f5=0.6, lambda_f6=0.3; formant amplitudes lambda_a1=4, lambda_a2=2.4, lambda_a3=1.2, lambda_a4=0.9, lambda_a5=0.6, lambda_a6=0.3; broadband filter lambda_fu=10, lambda_au=4, lambda_bu=4.
Overall ECoG decoder loss weights lambda_1=1.2, lambda_2=0.1, lambda_3=1.
Adam optimizer, lr=1e-3, beta1=0.9, beta2=0.999.
Training split: 350 trials train, 50 trials test per participant; word-level cross-validation with 10 held-out unique words, 5 folds.

subsections.title: Evaluation Metrics
content_bullets: Pearson Correlation Coefficient (PCC) between original and decoded spectrograms.
Short-Time Objective Intelligibility (STOI+) with one-third octave band analysis (15 bands, lowest center 150 Hz, highest ~4.3 kHz, P=16, M=2, N=30).
Wilcoxon signed-rank test for paired comparisons; Wilcoxon rank-sum test for unpaired comparisons (hemispheres).

subsections.title: Occlusion-based Contribution Analysis
content_bullets: Zero out each electrode and measure reduction in decoding PCC on testing data.
Projected onto MNI anatomical coordinates, averaged across participants, normalized by local grid density.

subsections.title: Model architectures and causality
content_bullets: ResNet non-causal PCC=0.804, causal PCC=0.798.
SWIN non-causal PCC=0.793, causal PCC=0.796.
LSTM non-causal PCC=0.757, causal PCC=0.713.
ResNet non-causal vs causal Wilcoxon p=0.1022; SWIN p=0.2794; LSTM p=0.0007.
Causal ResNet vs causal SWIN Wilcoxon p=0.1413.

subsections.title: Speech parameter reconstruction
content_bullets: Voice weight PCC=0.834.
Loudness PCC=0.545.
Pitch f0 PCC=0.907.
First formant f1 PCC=0.827.
Second formant f2 PCC=0.895.

subsections.title: Left vs right hemisphere
content_bullets: Right hemisphere ResNet PCC=0.790, SWIN PCC=0.781.
No significant PCC difference with left hemisphere: ResNet rank-sum p=0.312; SWIN p=0.325.
STOI+ tests: ResNet p=0.108; SWIN p=0.092.

subsections.title: Electrode density
content_bullets: Hybrid-density (n=5) vs low-density (n=43) grids: similar PCC; ResNet slightly better on LD grids.
For 4 of 5 HB participants, using only LD-in-HB subset does not significantly change PCC.
48% of participants show PCC in 0.80-0.90 range.

subsections.title: Cortical contribution analysis
content_bullets: Non-causal models show strong auditory cortex (STG) contribution; causal models rely on frontal regions.
Right and left causal models show similar ventral sensorimotor contributions.

sections.title: Discussion
content_bullets: PCC range 0.52-0.91 across participants; causal ResNet mean 0.798, median 0.81, range 0.64-0.91.
48% of participants reach 0.80-0.90 decoding PCC.
Causal models remove auditory feedback over-reliance; important for real-time BCI.
Speech parameter space preserves participant-specific acoustics unlike kinematic representations.
Limitations: requires paired speech-ECoG training data, assumes grid electrodes, focuses on word-level decoding.
Potential for right-hemisphere prostheses in expressive aphasia patients.

sections.title: Conclusion
content_bullets: Open-source, reproducible, causal ECoG-to-speech pipeline with naturalistic synthesis.
Evidence that right hemisphere and low-density grids support robust decoding.
Pipeline with interchangeable decoders supports community comparisons.
